title: Deploying Translation Systems
concept: Production translation requires handling tokenization for multiple languages, managing vocabulary size, and minimizing decoding latency.

exercises.prompt: What is the main challenge when deploying translation systems to production?

options.text: How to translate text quickly and memory-efficiently
How to print books in different languages
How to write a dictionary by hand

exercises.explain: Deploying translation systems requires balancing language rules, dictionary size, and generation speed (latency).

cards.title: Translating at Warp Speed
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how apps like Google Translate convert your voice to another language in milliseconds? It is not just about being smart; it is about being incredibly fast and efficient behind the scenes!

cards.title: Deploying Translation Systems
cards.body: Deploying translation systems in the real world requires managing three key areas: Tokenization (splitting text into sub-words across languages), Vocabulary Size (keeping the word dictionary small enough for server memory), and Decoding Latency (making sure the translation generates quickly, word-by-word).

cards.title: The Universal Airport
cards.body: Imagine running an international airport. Instead of hiring thousands of translators for every single language, you use a universal passport system. By breaking down words into common sub-word 'passports' (like 'trans' and 'late'), you can translate almost any language using a compact, manageable vocabulary.

cards.title: Putting it into Practice
cards.body: When translating 'unbelievable' into Spanish, the model doesn't store the whole word. It tokenizes it into ['un', 'believ', 'able'], translates it, and outputs Spanish sub-words. By doing this, it avoids running out of memory while translating rare words instantly.

cards.title: The Giant Dictionary Myth
cards.body: Many people think translation systems store every word of every language in a giant dictionary. In reality, they use sub-word tokenization to build new words out of smaller pieces, allowing them to translate words they have never even seen before!

cards.title: Whole Words vs. Sub-words
cards.body: See how a production system simplifies input text compared to an inefficient system that stores whole words.

right.text: Uses small pieces (tokens) to build any word. Fits easily in memory.

left.text: Requires billions of unique words stored in memory. Often fails on new words.

exercises.prompt: Why do we use sub-word tokenization in production translation systems?

options.text: It forces the model to only translate English words.
It lets the model handle rare words using a smaller, shared vocabulary.
It makes the translation process much slower.
It requires storing every word in existence.

exercises.explain: Sub-word tokenization breaks rare words down into smaller, common pieces, which prevents the system from running out of memory.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Minimizing decoding latency is important for real-time translation apps.
exercises.explain: Low decoding latency is critical for real-time translation so users do not have to wait for their results.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the speed of a translation system.
exercises.sentence: To make translations feel instant, developers must minimize decoding ______.
exercises.explain: Decoding latency measures the time it takes for a model to generate the translated words. Keeping it low ensures users get instant results.

exercises.prompt: Identify which user is describing the correct AI approach to handling large vocabularies in translation:

options.text: User A: 'We need a dictionary that stores all ten million words in every language.'
User B: 'We should break words down into smaller pieces like "play" and "ing" to save space.'
User C: 'We should just translate letter by letter, which is the fastest way.'

exercises.explain: The AI's response uses sub-word tokens ('play' + 'ing') to build words efficiently, whereas the human's response assumes we must store every whole word, which is impossible.

exercises.prompt: Your team is building a global translation app that needs to run fast on server systems while supporting dozens of languages. What is the best strategy for deploying this system?

options.text: Tokenize words into sub-units, cap the vocabulary size, and optimize decoding latency.
Train a separate model for every language pair and run them all on the user's phone.
Only translate English words and reject all foreign characters to save memory.
Store every possible word in the world in a massive dictionary table.

exercises.explain: To successfully deploy translation systems, you must handle multiple languages by splitting words into sub-word tokens, limiting the vocabulary size to fit in memory, and minimizing decoding latency for fast responses.
